text.title: Core Statistical Concepts for A/B Testing
text.summary: Null hypothesis, p-value, alpha, power, confidence intervals, and the Type I/II error trade-off.

rule.statement: State H0 (no difference) and H1 (difference exists) before collecting any data.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc hypothesis selection inflates false positive rates; the null hypothesis is the default claim the test must disprove.

example.note: H0: conversion_rate_A = conversion_rate_B; H1: conversion_rate_A != conversion_rate_B (two-tailed)

rule.statement: Set alpha = 0.05 before launch; reject H0 only when p-value is strictly less than alpha.
rule.rationale: Alpha defines acceptable false positive rate. p-value is the probability of seeing this result (or more extreme) if H0 were true — not the probability that B is better.

list.item: 0.05 <= p < 0.10: weak evidence, collect more data
p >= 0.10: insufficient evidence to reject H0

rule.statement: Target 80% power; calculate required sample size from baseline rate, MDE, alpha, and power before starting.
rule.rationale: 80% power means a 20% chance of missing a real effect (Type II error). Running underpowered tests wastes traffic and produces untrustworthy null results.

example.note: Running with 500 per variant when 57,600 are needed gives ~6% power — essentially useless for detecting a 0.5% absolute lift from 10% baseline.

rule.statement: Report the 95% CI for the difference alongside p-value; do not ship a result where the CI lower bound is economically irrelevant.
rule.rationale: A significant p-value with CI [0.01%, 4%] is statistically real but the lower bound may not justify shipping costs. CI width reflects data quantity — wider = need more data.

example.note: Conversion A=5.0%, B=5.8%, difference=+0.8%, 95% CI=[0.2%, 1.4%] — zero not in interval, significant and economically meaningful.

rule.statement: Alpha controls Type I error (false positive); power controls Type II error (false negative) — tune both for the business cost of each mistake.
rule.rationale: Shipping a bad change (Type I) is costly in high-traffic funnels. Missing a real improvement (Type II) is costly when testing is expensive. High-stakes tests warrant alpha=0.01 or power=90%.

section.title: Bayesian vs Frequentist
section.p: Frequentist p-values say "if H0 is true, how likely is this data?" Bayesian posteriors say "given this data, how likely is B better?" For teams that want intuitive probability statements, Bayesian A/B testing is a valid alternative but requires prior assumptions.
